title: Copy, Replace, Rename, Delete, Batch, and Concurrency · Runtime Contract: Delete Version Marker
summary: A rename is presented as atomic, replacement loses metadata, delete hides a live version, and concurrent writers silently overwrite each other. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the delete version marker increment in conditional object-mutation protocol. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish unversioned deletion, current-version delete markers, version-specific deletion, retention, legal hold, and recovery.
Use validators, version IDs, idempotency records, or application locks to detect stale concurrent mutation and expose 409 or 412 meaning.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Delete Version Marker · Theory Delete Version Marker
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 9857 during a private media release. Build deterministic Go evidence to model a non-atomic version-aware copy, validate, and delete rename protocol; keep browser execution deterministic and move AWS claims to an authorized transfer gate; then introduce a duplicate event or ambiguous response and reconcile without duplicate effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Delete Version Marker contract for the supplied data.
